---
title: "Week 08 — Thursday Workshop: Project Progress, Peer Review & Method Justification"
format: html
---

Week: 8

Topic: Project Progress, Peer Review & Method Justification

# Workshop Overview

| Part | Activity | Time |
|------|----------|------|
| **Part 1** | Timeline Self-Assessment | 15 min |
| **Part 2** | Peer Feedback — Project Pairs | 25 min |
| **Part 3** | Method Justification — Written Exercise | 15 min |
| **Part 4** | Structured Work Time | 20 min |
| **Part 5** | Closing — One Word Check-Out | ~5 min |
| | **Total** | **~75–80 min** |

---

## Part 1: Timeline Self-Assessment (15 min)

### Step 1 — Individual Review (5 min)

Open your project README and review your **Expected Timeline**.

Answer the following:

::: {.callout-tip}
## Timeline Check Prompts

**What did you plan to have done by Week 8?**

<br><br>

**What have you actually accomplished?**

<br><br>

**Traffic light rating:**

⬜ 🟢 Green — On track or ahead  
⬜ 🟡 Yellow — Slightly behind; I know what I need to do  
⬜ 🔴 Red — Significantly behind; need help re-planning

**If Yellow or Red: one concrete thing I will do this week:**

<br><br>

**Updated timeline for remaining weeks:**

| Week | Goal |
|------|------|
| Week 9 | |
| Week 10 | |
| Week 11 | |
| Week 12 | |
:::

### Step 2 — Share with Neighbor (5 min)

Share your traffic light color and your "one thing" with the person next to you. Offer one suggestion or piece of encouragement.

### Step 3 — Whole Class Pulse (5 min)

Show of hands:

- 🟢 How many are on track?
- 🟡 How many are slightly behind?
- 🔴 How many need to re-plan?

Dr. Molina will address the most common challenges and offer brief guidance.

---

## Part 2: Peer Feedback — Project Pairs (25 min)

Pair with someone **outside** your immediate research area.

**⏱ 12 minutes per person (24 min + 1 min transition)**

### Presenter Role (12 min)

| Time | Task |
|------|------|
| 3 min | Explain your project: question, data, and why it matters |
| 3 min | Show your current work: code, figures, or written sections |
| 6 min | Receive feedback from your reviewer |

### Reviewer Feedback Guide

Use this structure when reviewing your partner's project:

::: {.callout-note}
## Peer Feedback Guide

**Strengths** (at least one):
> "One thing that is working well is..."

**Triangle Check**:
> "Based on what I heard, the Method–Question–Data Triangle [does / does not yet] align because..."

**One Idea**:
> "One thing you might consider is..."

**The Hard Question**:
> Ask one question the presenter may not have considered yet. Examples:
> - "What distribution does your response variable follow, and does your model assume that?"
> - "What happens to your conclusions if [assumption] is violated?"
> - "Have you checked for [overdispersion / influential points / non-independence]?"
:::

### After Both Rounds

Write down:

1. **One piece of feedback I received that I will act on:**

<br><br>

2. **One idea I gave my partner that also applies to my own work:**

<br><br>

---

## Part 3: Method Justification — Written Exercise (15 min)

Write a **150–250 word paragraph** answering the following question:

> **"Why is [your method] the appropriate analysis for your research question, given your data?"**

Your paragraph must address:

::: {.callout-tip}
## Writing Checklist

⬜ What is your response variable and what distribution does it follow?  
⬜ What is your data structure (independent, nested, repeated, blocked)?  
⬜ Why is a simpler analysis insufficient?  
⬜ Why is a more complex analysis not warranted?  
⬜ What assumptions does your method make, and how will you check them?
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Required References

Your paragraph must reference:

1. The **Method–Question–Data Triangle** (Week 3) — explicitly connect your method to your question and data type
2. At least **one philosophical concept from Week 2** — e.g., "All models are wrong, some are useful" (Box), or your choice of inferential framework
:::

**Optional:** 1–2 volunteers read their paragraph aloud; class provides quick feedback (3 min).

---

## Part 4: Structured Work Time (20 min)

Work on your project. Dr. Molina will circulate.

**Suggested priorities by traffic light color:**

| Color | Focus |
|-------|-------|
| 🟢 Green | Run your planned analyses; create or refine figures |
| 🟡 Yellow | Focus exclusively on your "one thing" from Part 1 |
| 🔴 Red | Meet with Dr. Molina — re-plan your timeline now |

::: {.callout-important}
## ⏱ Before You Leave: Commit to Git

Before the end of class, commit your current work with a **meaningful commit message**.

Good examples:
- `"Add exploratory plots for soil moisture data"`
- `"Fit initial lmer model for yield ~ treatment + (1|block)"`
- `"Draft methods paragraph for Week 8 justification exercise"`

Bad examples:
- `"update"`
- `"stuff"`
- `"asdfgh"`

Your commit history is part of your project record. Future-you (and your committee) will thank present-you.
:::

---

## Part 5: Closing — One Word Check-Out (~5 min)

Go around the room. Each person says **one word** describing how they feel about their project right now.

::: {.callout-note}
## Instructor Note: SoTL Data

One-word check-outs provide data on the **affective dimension** of learning — confidence, anxiety, engagement, momentum. Tracking these words across weeks can reveal when students feel most uncertain (often before major milestones) and when momentum builds. Consider recording them in a simple log.
:::

---

## Workshop Summary

| Part | Activity | Time |
|------|----------|------|
| **1** | Timeline Self-Assessment (individual → neighbor → class) | 15 min |
| **2** | Peer Feedback — Project Pairs (12 min per person) | 25 min |
| **3** | Method Justification — Written paragraph (150–250 words) | 15 min |
| **4** | Structured Work Time + Git commit | 20 min |
| **5** | One Word Check-Out | ~5 min |
| | **Total** | **~75–80 min** |
